title: Survival Analysis with brms and shrinkr
text: vignette demonstrates hierarchical shrinkage survival analysis using classic veteran lung cancer dataset. explore key clinical question: treatment effect vary lung cancer cell type? Rather treating cell type-specific treatment effects fixed interaction terms, model random effects drawn common distribution. hierarchical structure allows us : Borrow strength across cell types, especially small subgroups Estimate overall mean treatment effect (mu) Quantify heterogeneity treatment effects (tau) Shrink extreme subgroup estimates toward group mean compare three modeling approaches: Two-stage (brms + shrinkr): fit Cox model brms, apply hierarchical shrinkage shrinkr Full hierarchical (brms): fit hierarchical Cox model one step Two-stage (frequentist + shrinkr): use Cox model estimates survival::coxph(), apply shrinkage two-stage brms workflow produces nearly identical results full hierarchical model, making easy explore alternative hierarchical priors without repeatedly refitting Stage 1 model. model-fitting steps computationally intensive evaluated routine package checks. code needed reproduce analysis shown .

title: Survival Analysis with brms and shrinkr
code: library(shrinkr) library(brms) library(tidybayes) library(distributional) library(tidyverse) library(survival) library(posterior) library(patchwork)  theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))  cell_types <- c("squamous", "smallcell", "adeno", "large")  prior_specs <- list(   very_strong = list(name = "Very Strong", scale = 0.1),   strong = list(name = "Strong", scale = 0.25),   moderate = list(name = "Moderate", scale = 0.5),   weak = list(name = "Weak", scale = 1.0),   very_weak = list(name = "Very Weak", scale = 2.0) )

title: Survival Analysis with brms and shrinkr
text: Variables: time: survival time (days) status: death indicator (1 = died) trt: treatment (1 = standard, 2 = test) celltype: cancer type (squamous, smallcell, adeno, large) karno: Karnofsky score (performance status) age: age years dataset contains 137 patients across four cell types, varying sample sizes.
code: data(veteran, package = "survival")  head(veteran) #>   trt celltype time status karno diagtime age prior #> 1   1 squamous   72      1    60        7  69     0 #> 2   1 squamous  411      1    70        5  64    10 #> 3   1 squamous  228      1    60        3  38     0 #> 4   1 squamous  126      1    60        9  63    10 #> 5   1 squamous  118      1    70       11  65    10 #> 6   1 squamous   10      1    20        5  49     0 table(veteran$celltype, veteran$trt) #>             #>              1  2 #>   squamous  15 20 #>   smallcell 30 18 #>   adeno      9 18 #>   large     15 12  veteran %>%   group_by(celltype, trt) %>%   summarise(     n = n(),     deaths = sum(status),     median_time = median(time),     .groups = "drop"   ) #> # A tibble: 8 × 5 #>   celltype    trt     n deaths median_time #>   <fct>     <dbl> <int>  <dbl>       <dbl> #> 1 squamous      1    15     13       100   #> 2 squamous      2    20     18       156.  #> 3 smallcell     1    30     28        53   #> 4 smallcell     2    18     17        27   #> 5 adeno         1     9      9        92   #> 6 adeno         2    18     17        49.5 #> 7 large         1    15     14       177   #> 8 large         2    12     12        82

title: Survival Analysis with brms and shrinkr
text: begin fitting Cox proportional hazards model allows treatment effect vary cell type. stage estimate subgroup-specific treatment effects without adding hierarchical shrinkage across cell types. hierarchical regularization introduced Stage 2. model : Estimates separate log hazard ratio test treatment cell type Adjusts baseline performance status (karno) age Leaves pooling across cell types second-stage hierarchical model Results:
code: brms_uninformative <- brm(   time | cens(1 - status) ~ trt:celltype + karno + age,   data = veteran,   family = cox(),   chains = 4,   iter = 4000,   warmup = 1000,   seed = 123 )  brms_uninformative_summary <- capture.output(print(summary(brms_uninformative))) cat(brms_uninformative_summary, sep = "\n") #>  Family: cox  #>   Links: mu = log  #> Formula: time | cens(1 - status) ~ trt:celltype + karno + age  #>    Data: veteran (Number of observations: 137)  #>   Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 4000; warmup = 1000; thin = 1; #>          total post-warmup draws = 12000 #>  #> Regression Coefficients: #>                       Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI #> Intercept                 4.16      0.74     2.70 #> karno                    -0.03      0.01    -0.04 #> age                      -0.01      0.01    -0.02 #> trt:celltypesquamous     -0.12      0.21    -0.52 #> trt:celltypesmallcell     0.51      0.23     0.05 #> trt:celltypeadeno         0.54      0.20     0.13 #> trt:celltypelarge         0.16      0.23    -0.31 #>                       u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS #> Intercept                 5.61 1.00     9000     8957 #> karno                    -0.02 1.00    11647     9539 #> age                       0.01 1.00     9875     8676 #> trt:celltypesquamous      0.29 1.00     5515     7429 #> trt:celltypesmallcell     0.95 1.00     5310     7315 #> trt:celltypeadeno         0.94 1.00     5398     7230 #> trt:celltypelarge         0.61 1.00     5399     7077 #>  #> Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS #> and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential #> scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).

title: Survival Analysis with brms and shrinkr
text: brms_posteriors named list containing posterior draws cell type.
code: brms_posteriors <- brms_uninformative %>%   spread_draws(`b_trt:celltypesquamous`, `b_trt:celltypesmallcell`,                `b_trt:celltypeadeno`, `b_trt:celltypelarge`) %>%   select(-c(.chain, .iteration, .draw)) %>%   pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "celltype", values_to = "value") %>%   mutate(celltype = gsub("b_trt:celltype", "", celltype)) %>%   group_by(celltype) %>%   summarise(draws = list(matrix(value, ncol = 1)), .groups = "drop") %>%   deframe()

text: fit_mixture() function approximates subgroup posterior mixture Gaussian components. creates flexible representation Stage 1 posterior can passed shrink().  Understanding mixture approximation: posterior approximated weighted sum Gaussian components number components chosen separately cell type allows approximation capture skewness heavier tails needed
code: mix_brms <- fit_mixture(brms_posteriors, K_max = 3, verbose = TRUE) print(mix_brms) plot(mix_brms, draws = brms_posteriors)

title: Survival Analysis with brms and shrinkr
text: Results: Interpreting shrinkage: Cell type-specific estimates pulled toward overall mean (mu) amount shrinkage depends tau, -cell-type heterogeneity Smaller tau implies stronger pooling Larger tau implies weaker pooling
code: priors_moderate <- list(   mu = dist_normal(0, 1),   tau = dist_truncated(dist_normal(0, 0.5), lower = 0) ) fit_twostage_brms <- shrink(   mixture = mix_brms,   hierarchical_priors = priors_moderate,   chains = 4,   iter = 4000,   warmup = 1000,   seed = 456 )  moderate_brms_output <- capture.output(print(fit_twostage_brms)) cat(moderate_brms_output, sep = "\n") #> # A tibble: 3 × 7 #>   variable     mean    sd    q2.5   q50 q97.5  rhat #>   <chr>       <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 mu          0.246 0.265 -0.272  0.245 0.757  1.00 #> 2 tau         0.361 0.176  0.123  0.324 0.812  1.00 #> 3 tau_squared 0.161 0.177  0.0150 0.105 0.659  1.00

title: Survival Analysis with brms and shrinkr
text: comparison, fit corresponding one-stage hierarchical Cox model directly brms. Results:
code: brms_hierarchical <- brm(   time | cens(1 - status) ~ trt + (0 + trt | celltype) + karno + age,   data = veteran,   family = cox(),   prior = c(     prior(normal(0, 1), class = b, coef = "trt"),     prior(normal(0, 0.5), class = sd, group = "celltype", lb = 0)   ),   chains = 4,   iter = 4000,   warmup = 1000,   seed = 123 )  brms_hierarchical_summary <- capture.output(print(summary(brms_hierarchical)))  brms_hier_effects <- brms_hierarchical %>%   spread_draws(r_celltype[celltype, term], b_trt) %>%   filter(term == "trt") %>%   mutate(theta = b_trt + r_celltype) %>%   group_by(celltype) %>%   summarise(     hr_mean = exp(mean(theta)),     hr_lower = exp(quantile(theta, 0.025)),     hr_upper = exp(quantile(theta, 0.975)),     .groups = "drop"   ) cat(brms_hierarchical_summary, sep = "\n") #>  Family: cox  #>   Links: mu = log  #> Formula: time | cens(1 - status) ~ trt + (0 + trt | celltype) + karno + age  #>    Data: veteran (Number of observations: 137)  #>   Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 4000; warmup = 1000; thin = 1; #>          total post-warmup draws = 12000 #>  #> Multilevel Hyperparameters: #> ~celltype (Number of levels: 4)  #>         Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat #> sd(trt)     0.36      0.18     0.13     0.80 1.00 #>         Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS #> sd(trt)     4188     6657 #>  #> Regression Coefficients: #>           Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat #> Intercept     4.11      0.74     2.66     5.55 1.00 #> trt           0.26      0.26    -0.24     0.79 1.00 #> karno        -0.03      0.01    -0.04    -0.02 1.00 #> age          -0.01      0.01    -0.02     0.01 1.00 #>           Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS #> Intercept    11705     9033 #> trt           5386     6740 #> karno        13722     9846 #> age          11879     7469 #>  #> Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS #> and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential #> scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).

title: Survival Analysis with brms and shrinkr
text: Comparison treatment effects (log HR scale) Key observations: two-stage brms + shrinkr full hierarchical brms fits nearly identical supports equivalence two formulations example frequentist Stage 1 approach differs modestly first-stage estimates differ approaches show shrinkage toward common mean
code: theta_brms <- summary(fit_twostage_brms)$theta %>%   transmute(     celltype = group,     twostage_brms = mean   )  theta_freq <- summary(fit_twostage_freq)$theta %>%   transmute(     celltype = group,     twostage_freq = mean   )  comparison <- brms_hier_effects %>%   transmute(     celltype,     full_hier_brms = log(hr_mean)   ) %>%   left_join(theta_brms, by = "celltype") %>%   left_join(theta_freq, by = "celltype") %>%   mutate(     diff_two_stage_vs_full = twostage_brms - full_hier_brms   ) knitr::kable(   comparison[, 1:4],   digits = 3,   caption = "Comparison of treatment effects (log HR scale)" )

title: Survival Analysis with brms and shrinkr
code: theta_brms_plot <- summary(fit_twostage_brms)$theta %>%   mutate(     approach = "Two-Stage (brms + shrinkr)",     hr_mean = exp(mean),     hr_lower = exp(q2.5),     hr_upper = exp(q97.5),     celltype = group   ) %>%   select(celltype, approach, hr_mean, hr_lower, hr_upper)  theta_freq_plot <- summary(fit_twostage_freq)$theta %>%   mutate(     approach = "Two-Stage (Frequentist + shrinkr)",     hr_mean = exp(mean),     hr_lower = exp(q2.5),     hr_upper = exp(q97.5),     celltype = group   ) %>%   select(celltype, approach, hr_mean, hr_lower, hr_upper)  all_approaches <- bind_rows(   theta_brms_plot,   brms_hier_effects %>% mutate(approach = "Full Hierarchical (brms)"),   theta_freq_plot ) %>%   mutate(     approach = factor(approach, levels = c(       "Two-Stage (brms + shrinkr)",       "Full Hierarchical (brms)",       "Two-Stage (Frequentist + shrinkr)"     ))   ) ggplot(all_approaches, aes(x = celltype, y = hr_mean, color = approach)) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +   geom_pointrange(     aes(ymin = hr_lower, ymax = hr_upper),     position = position_dodge(width = 0.5),     size = 0.8   ) +   scale_y_log10() +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   labs(     title = "Comparison of Three Modeling Approaches",     subtitle = "Treatment effects by cell type (hazard ratios)",     x = "Cell Type",     y = "Hazard Ratio (log scale)",     color = "Approach"   ) +   theme(     legend.position = "bottom",     panel.grid.minor = element_blank()   )

title: Survival Analysis with brms and shrinkr
text: main advantage two-stage framework can explore many hierarchical priors Stage 2 without refitting Stage 1 survival model.
code: prior_summary <- tibble(   Strength = c("Very Strong", "Strong", "Moderate", "Weak", "Very Weak"),   Prior = c(     "Half-Normal(0, 0.1)",     "Half-Normal(0, 0.25)",     "Half-Normal(0, 0.5)",     "Half-Normal(0, 1.0)",     "Half-Normal(0, 2.0)"   ),   Scale = c(0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0),   Interpretation = c(     "Very similar effects expected",     "Similar effects expected",     "Moderate heterogeneity allowed",     "Substantial differences allowed",     "Large differences allowed"   ) )  knitr::kable(prior_summary) all_priors <- list(   very_strong = list(     mu = dist_normal(0, 1),     tau = dist_truncated(dist_normal(0, 0.1), lower = 0)   ),   strong = list(     mu = dist_normal(0, 1),     tau = dist_truncated(dist_normal(0, 0.25), lower = 0)   ),   moderate = list(     mu = dist_normal(0, 1),     tau = dist_truncated(dist_normal(0, 0.5), lower = 0)   ),   weak = list(     mu = dist_normal(0, 1),     tau = dist_truncated(dist_normal(0, 1.0), lower = 0)   ),   very_weak = list(     mu = dist_normal(0, 1),     tau = dist_truncated(dist_normal(0, 2.0), lower = 0)   ) )  # --- brms fits --- sensitivity_fits_brms <- lapply(all_priors, function(prior) {   shrink(     mixture = mix_brms,     hierarchical_priors = prior,     chains = 4,     iter = 4000,     warmup = 1000   ) })  # --- frequentist fits --- sensitivity_fits_freq <- lapply(all_priors, function(prior) {   shrink(     mle = trt_effects,     var_matrix = trt_vcov,     hierarchical_priors = prior,     chains = 4,     iter = 4000,     warmup = 1000   ) })  # --- summaries --- sensitivity_summaries <- c(   purrr::imap(sensitivity_fits_brms, function(fit, nm) {     summ <- summary(fit)     list(       theta_summary = summ$theta,       mu_tau_summary = summ$mu_tau,       print_output = capture.output(print(fit))     )   }),   purrr::imap(sensitivity_fits_freq, function(fit, nm) {     summ <- summary(fit)     list(       theta_summary = summ$theta,       mu_tau_summary = summ$mu_tau,       print_output = capture.output(print(fit))     )   }) )  # --- name them clearly --- names(sensitivity_summaries) <- c(   paste0(names(all_priors), "_brms"),   paste0(names(all_priors), "_freq") )

title: Survival Analysis with brms and shrinkr
code: tau_seq <- seq(0, 3, length.out = 200)  prior_densities <- lapply(names(prior_specs), function(spec_name) {   spec <- prior_specs[[spec_name]]   tibble(     tau = tau_seq,     density = dnorm(tau_seq, 0, spec$scale) * 2,     prior_strength = spec$name,     scale = spec$scale   ) }) %>%   bind_rows() %>%   mutate(     prior_strength = factor(prior_strength, levels = c(       "Very Strong", "Strong", "Moderate", "Weak", "Very Weak"     ))   )  ggplot(prior_densities, aes(x = tau, y = density, color = prior_strength)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "RdYlBu", direction = -1) +   labs(     title = "Prior Densities for the Heterogeneity Parameter (tau)",     subtitle = "Half-Normal(0, sigma) priors with increasing scale",     x = "tau",     y = "Density",     color = "Prior Strength"   ) +   theme(legend.position = "right")

title: Survival Analysis with brms and shrinkr
text: Interpretation: Stronger priors constrain tau toward smaller values produce shrinkage Weaker priors allow -cell-type variation posterior tau stabilizes prior becomes less restrictive
code: tau_results <- lapply(names(sensitivity_summaries), function(fit_name) {   summary_obj <- sensitivity_summaries[[fit_name]]   prior_name <- sub("_(brms|freq)$", "", fit_name)   approach <- if (grepl("_brms$", fit_name)) "brms + shrinkr" else "Frequentist + shrinkr"    summary_obj$mu_tau_summary %>%     filter(parameter == "tau") %>%     mutate(       prior_strength = prior_specs[[prior_name]]$name,       prior_scale = prior_specs[[prior_name]]$scale,       approach = approach     ) }) %>%   bind_rows() %>%   mutate(     prior_strength = factor(       prior_strength,       levels = c("Very Strong", "Strong", "Moderate", "Weak", "Very Weak")     )   )  if (all(c("q2.5", "q97.5") %in% names(tau_results))) {   tau_results <- tau_results %>%     mutate(lower = `q2.5`, upper = `q97.5`) } else if (all(c("q5", "q95") %in% names(tau_results))) {   tau_results <- tau_results %>%     mutate(lower = q5, upper = q95) } else {   stop(     "Could not find interval columns in sensitivity_summaries$mu_tau_summary. ",     "Available columns are: ",     paste(names(tau_results), collapse = ", ")   ) }  ggplot(tau_results, aes(x = prior_scale, y = mean, color = approach)) +   geom_point(size = 3, position = position_dodge(width = 0.1)) +   geom_errorbar(     aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper),     width = 0.1,     linewidth = 1,     position = position_dodge(width = 0.1)   ) +   geom_line(aes(group = approach), position = position_dodge(width = 0.1)) +   scale_x_log10(breaks = c(0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0)) +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +   labs(     title = "Sensitivity of the Heterogeneity Parameter (tau)",     subtitle = "How prior scale affects the estimated between-cell-type variation",     x = "Prior Scale (log scale)",     y = "Posterior tau",     color = "Stage 1 Approach"   ) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Survival Analysis with brms and shrinkr
code: theta_sensitivity <- lapply(names(sensitivity_summaries), function(fit_name) {   summary_obj <- sensitivity_summaries[[fit_name]]   prior_name <- sub("_(brms|freq)$", "", fit_name)   approach <- if (grepl("_brms$", fit_name)) "brms + shrinkr" else "Frequentist + shrinkr"    summary_obj$theta_summary %>%     mutate(       prior_strength = prior_specs[[prior_name]]$name,       prior_scale = prior_specs[[prior_name]]$scale,       approach = approach,       hr_mean = exp(mean),       hr_lower = exp(q2.5),       hr_upper = exp(q97.5)     ) }) %>%   bind_rows() %>%   mutate(     prior_strength = factor(prior_strength, levels = c(       "Very Strong", "Strong", "Moderate", "Weak", "Very Weak"     ))   ) ggplot(theta_sensitivity, aes(x = prior_scale, y = hr_mean, color = approach)) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +   geom_point(size = 2, position = position_dodge(width = 0.1)) +   geom_errorbar(     aes(ymin = hr_lower, ymax = hr_upper),     width = 0.1,     position = position_dodge(width = 0.1)   ) +   geom_line(aes(group = approach), position = position_dodge(width = 0.1)) +   facet_wrap(~group, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +   scale_x_log10(breaks = c(0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0)) +   scale_y_log10() +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +   labs(     title = "Sensitivity Analysis: Cell Type-Specific Treatment Effects",     subtitle = "How the prior scale affects hazard ratio estimates",     x = "Prior Scale (log scale)",     y = "Hazard Ratio (log scale)",     color = "Stage 1 Approach"   ) +   theme(     legend.position = "bottom",     panel.grid.minor = element_blank()   )

title: Survival Analysis with brms and shrinkr
text: two-stage brms + shrinkr workflow closely matches full hierarchical brms analysis example. two-stage approach modular: fit survival model , explore many hierarchical priors efficiently. Sensitivity analysis becomes straightforward Stage 2 can rerun without refitting Stage 1. fit_mixture() provides flexible approximation subgroup posteriors, shrink() adds hierarchical regularization top approximation.

title: Federated Learning with shrinkr
text: Federated learning enables collaborative analysis across multiple sites without centralizing data. critical : Data leave institutional firewalls (HIPAA, GDPR) site proprietary sensitive data Centralizing data logistically infeasible want preserve patient privacy shrinkr’s two-stage architecture naturally enables federated learning: Data never leaves sites, statistical summaries shared. Important: CLT Assumption Summary Statistics sites share summary statistics (means + SEs) rather full posteriors, analysis relies Bayesian Central Limit Theorem. assumes posteriors approximately normal, requires: Adequate sample sizes site Parameters interior (near boundaries) Well-behaved likelihood functions Regular posterior geometry Always verify posterior normality using summary statistics! doubt, share full posteriors send mixture approximations, example sending fit_mixture() output.
code: Site 1: Stage 1 model -> Posterior samples (or summaries) Site 2: Stage 1 model -> Posterior samples (or summaries)  } -> Central Site 3: Stage 1 model -> Posterior samples (or summaries)  }   Coordinator    ...                                                     }   applies Site K: Stage 1 model -> Posterior samples (or summaries)  }   Stage 2 shrinkage library(shrinkr) library(distributional) library(posterior) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2) library(tidyr)

text: site fits: logit(mortality)=β0+β1(age)+β2(severity_score)+β3(comorbidities) \text{logit}(\text{mortality}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1(\text{age}) + \beta_2(\text{severity\_score}) + \beta_3(\text{comorbidities}) Parameter interest: β1\beta_1 (age effect mortality) want site-specific estimates want borrow strength across network

title: Federated Learning with shrinkr
text: practice, happens behind site’s firewall. simulate: Key observations: Smaller sites, example Community Hospital, wider intervals Point estimates vary across sites rural site fewer patients least precise
code: set.seed(1104)  # True network-level parameters (unknown in practice) true_mu_age <- 0.05    # log-OR per year true_tau_age <- 0.015  # between-site heterogeneity true_site_effects <- rnorm(6, true_mu_age, true_tau_age)  # Simulate Stage 1: Each site fits their model independently # In reality: glm(), stan_glm(), or other Bayesian logistic regression  site_posteriors <- list() site_sample_sizes <- hospitals$n_patients  for(i in 1:6) {   # Posterior for age coefficient beta_1   # SE inversely proportional to sqrt(sample size)   se_i <- 0.02 * sqrt(800 / site_sample_sizes[i])      site_posteriors[[hospitals$name[i]]] <- matrix(     rnorm(4000, true_site_effects[i], se_i),     ncol = 1   ) }  # Each site computes summaries site_summaries <- data.frame(   site = hospitals$name,   n_patients = site_sample_sizes,   beta_age_mean = sapply(site_posteriors, mean),   beta_age_se = sapply(site_posteriors, sd) ) %>%   mutate(     ci_lower = beta_age_mean - 1.96 * beta_age_se,     ci_upper = beta_age_mean + 1.96 * beta_age_se   )  print(site_summaries) #>                                      site n_patients beta_age_mean beta_age_se #> Metro General               Metro General       1500    0.04958522  0.01428679 #> County Regional           County Regional        800    0.05905738  0.01992675 #> University Medical     University Medical       2200    0.04482541  0.01246048 #> Community Hospital     Community Hospital        350    0.01077674  0.03047231 #> Veterans Affairs         Veterans Affairs       1100    0.06783668  0.01752091 #> Children's Specialty Children's Specialty        900    0.04679578  0.01888196 #>                          ci_lower   ci_upper #> Metro General         0.021583117 0.07758733 #> County Regional       0.020000961 0.09811381 #> University Medical    0.020402874 0.06924794 #> Community Hospital   -0.048948992 0.07050247 #> Veterans Affairs      0.033495692 0.10217766 #> Children's Specialty  0.009787133 0.08380442

title: Federated Learning with shrinkr
text: shrinkr package enables privacy-preserving federated learning two-stage design: Stage 1: Fit local models behind site’s firewall Check normality: Verify CLT assumptions using summaries Share: Full posteriors, mixtures, summaries Stage 2: Apply hierarchical shrinkage centrally Return: Improved site-specific estimates Key advantages: Data sovereignty preserved Flexible sharing options Handles non-normal posteriors via mixture approximation Improved estimates sites Proper uncertainty quantification Compatible privacy-focused workflows Critical decision: path? Path : Always valid handles posterior shape Path B: Valid posteriors approximately normal uncertain: Use Path

what: Stage 1: Fit Independent Models with Stan
title: Getting Started with shrinkr
text: First, let’s create synthetic trial data: Now write Stan model treatment--region interaction: Let’s examine got Stage 1: Stage 1 visualization:  Notice: Central widest interval (n=70) East narrowest (n=120). Estimates vary considerably — hierarchical shrinkage borrow strength across regions.
code: library(rstan) set.seed(1104)  true_mu <- 0.5 true_tau <- 0.3 true_effects <- c(0.45, 0.72, 0.38, 0.55, 0.61)  regions <- c("North", "South", "East", "West", "Central") n_per_region <- c(100, 80, 120, 90, 70)  trial_data <- lapply(seq_along(regions), function(i) {   n <- n_per_region[i]   data.frame(     region = regions[i],     treatment = rep(c(0, 1), each = n/2),     outcome = c(       rnorm(n/2, mean = 0, sd = 1),       rnorm(n/2, mean = true_effects[i], sd = 1)     )   ) }) %>% bind_rows() head(trial_data) table(trial_data$region, trial_data$treatment) #>   region treatment     outcome #> 1  North         0 -0.04382439 #> 2  North         0  0.64111730 #> 3  North         0 -0.33395868 #> 4  North         0 -2.60279243 #> 5  North         0  1.17838867 #> 6  North         0 -0.20705477 #>           #>            0  1 #>   Central 35 35 #>   East    60 60 #>   North   50 50 #>   South   40 40 #>   West    45 45 stan_code <- " data {   int<lower=0> N;   int<lower=1> G;   vector[N] y;   vector[N] treatment;   array[N] int<lower=1,upper=G> region; } parameters {   vector[G] beta_region;   real<lower=0> sigma; } model {   // IMPORTANT: Flat prior on beta_region - critical for the two-stage approach!   sigma ~ normal(0, 2);   for (n in 1:N) {     y[n] ~ normal(treatment[n] * beta_region[region[n]], sigma);   } } " regions <- c("North", "South", "East", "West", "Central") region_indices <- as.integer(factor(trial_data$region, levels = regions))  fit_stan <- stan(   model_code = stan_code,   data = list(     N = nrow(trial_data), G = length(regions),     y = trial_data$outcome, treatment = trial_data$treatment,     region = region_indices   ),   chains = 4, iter = 2000, warmup = 1000, refresh = 0, seed = 123 )  beta_draws <- rstan::extract(fit_stan, pars = "beta_region")$beta_region samples_list <- lapply(seq_along(regions), function(i) beta_draws[, i]) names(samples_list) <- regions samples <- lapply(samples_list, function(x) matrix(x, ncol = 1)) stage1_summary <- data.frame(   region = regions,   mean   = sapply(samples, mean),   sd     = sapply(samples, sd),   lower  = sapply(samples, function(x) quantile(x, 0.025)),   upper  = sapply(samples, function(x) quantile(x, 0.975)) )  print(stage1_summary) #>          region      mean        sd      lower     upper #> North     North 0.6040241 0.1405588 0.33617456 0.8799941 #> South     South 0.6000233 0.1540522 0.29866430 0.8924705 #> East       East 0.6152066 0.1265935 0.36515463 0.8666375 #> West       West 0.7049122 0.1494923 0.41466597 0.9940618 #> Central Central 0.3626256 0.1676080 0.02935275 0.6966270 ggplot(stage1_summary, aes(x = region, y = mean)) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +   geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper),                   size = 0.8, color = "steelblue") +   labs(     title    = "Stage 1: Independent Regional Estimates",     subtitle = "Each region analyzed separately with flat priors",     x = "Region", y = "Treatment Effect",     caption = "Points show posterior means; bars show 95% credible intervals"   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

text: Check prior implications fitting:  Check prior implies pairwise subgroup differences: implied_range measures max(theta) - min(theta) across groups draw. detailed view, prior_pairwise_differences() computes distribution |theta_i - theta_j| every pair groups. particularly useful calibrating whether prior places reasonable probability clinically meaningful differences.  prob_gt_0.5 prob_gt_1 columns summary show prior probability observing pairwise differences exceeding thresholds — useful assessing whether prior consistent clinical expectations subgroup heterogeneity.
code:
hierarchical_priors <- list(   mu  = dist_normal(0, 1),   tau = dist_truncated(dist_student_t(3, 0, 0.5), lower = 0) ) prior_pred <- sample_prior_predictive(   hierarchical_priors = hierarchical_priors,   n_groups = 5,   n_draws  = 1000 ) cat("Prior on tau (between-region SD):
") cat("  Median:", round(median(prior_pred$tau), 2), "
") cat("  95% interval:", round(quantile(prior_pred$tau, c(0.025, 0.975)), 2), "

") cat("  95% interval:", round(quantile(prior_pred$implied_range, c(0.025, 0.975)), 2), "

title: Getting Started with shrinkr
code: # 1. Write Stan model with FLAT PRIORS on parameters of interest stan_code <- " data {   int<lower=0> N;   int<lower=1> G;   vector[N] y;   vector[N] treatment;   array[N] int<lower=1,upper=G> group; } parameters {   vector[G] theta;  // Group-specific effects - NO PRIOR SPECIFIED   real<lower=0> sigma; } model {   sigma ~ normal(0, 2);   for (n in 1:N) {     y[n] ~ normal(treatment[n] * theta[group[n]], sigma);   } } "  # 2. Fit model once to get all group effects, extract posteriors group_indices <- as.integer(factor(data$group, levels = groups)) fit_stan <- stan(   model_code = stan_code,   data = list(N = nrow(data), G = length(groups),               y = data$y, treatment = data$treatment,               group = group_indices),   chains = 4, iter = 2000, warmup = 1000, refresh = 0 )  theta_draws <- extract(fit_stan)$theta samples <- lapply(seq_along(groups), function(i) matrix(theta_draws[, i], ncol = 1)) names(samples) <- groups  # 3. Fit mixture approximation and check quality mix <- fit_mixture(samples, K_max = 3) plot(mix, draws = samples)  # 4. Specify and check hierarchical priors priors <- list(   mu  = dist_normal(0, 5),   tau = dist_truncated(dist_student_t(3, 0, 1), lower = 0) ) plot(sample_prior_predictive(priors, n_groups = length(groups)))  # 5. Fit, extract, and visualize fit <- shrink(mixture = mix, hierarchical_priors = priors) plot(fit) summarize_theta(fit) extract_mu_tau(fit)

title: Getting Started with shrinkr
text: Stage 1 uses flat priors - critical! Don’t specify prior parameter ’ll shrink Mixture approximation - Allows shrinkr work posterior shape; check quality plot() Hierarchical priors - Applied Stage 2; use sample_prior_predictive() understand implications Adaptive shrinkage - Uncertain estimates borrow ; precise estimates borrow less Diagnostics matter - Always check mixture fit, prior-data agreement, MCMC convergence

title: Getting Started with shrinkr
text: Using informative priors Stage 1 - creates “double priors” breaks equivalence Skipping mixture quality checks - Poor approximation → biased shrinkage, run: plot(mix, draws = samples) Ignoring prior implications - prior might strong weak, run: sample_prior_predictive() plot() checking convergence - MCMC diagnostics apply Stage 2 , check: fit$diagnostics, Rhat, ESS

title: Meta-Analytic-Predictive (MAP) Priors with shrinkr and beastt
text: meta-analytic-predictive (MAP) prior summarizes several historical control arms single prior control mean new trial (Neuenschwander et al., 2010). random-effects meta-analysis: study-specific control means treated exchangeable draws common population, prior next trial posterior predictive distribution new, -yet-unobserved study. guard prior-data conflict, MAP robustified vague mixture component (Schmidli et al., 2014), historical data automatically -weighted disagree current trial. shrinkr beastt split work cleanly: shrinkr runs hierarchical meta-analysis across historical studies returns MAP distributional object. beastt robustifies MAP (robustify_norm()), combines internal control arm form posterior (calc_post_norm()), reports effective sample size (ESS). hand-single dist_normal object. use continuous outcome known within-arm SD, keeps every step conjugate.
code: library(shrinkr) library(beastt) library(distributional) library(tibble) library(ggplot2)  set.seed(1104) sigma_known <- 2   # known within-arm response SD (same in all arms)

title: Meta-Analytic-Predictive (MAP) Priors with shrinkr and beastt
text: MAP prior starts historical studies reported: control-arm mean standard error. six prior studies whose control means cluster fairly tightly around 10. known SD, standard error just sigma / sqrt(n).  current trial contributes control arm 70 patients:
code: n_hist <- c(58, 43, 62, 51, 47, 55) hist <- tibble(   study = paste0("H", 1:6),   n     = n_hist,   ybar  = c(9.9, 10.3, 9.7, 10.1, 10.4, 9.8),   se    = sigma_known / sqrt(n_hist) ) hist #> # A tibble: 6 × 4 #>   study     n  ybar    se #>   <chr> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 H1       58   9.9 0.263 #> 2 H2       43  10.3 0.305 #> 3 H3       62   9.7 0.254 #> 4 H4       51  10.1 0.280 #> 5 H5       47  10.4 0.292 #> 6 H6       55   9.8 0.270 ggplot(hist, aes(study, ybar)) +   geom_hline(yintercept = mean(hist$ybar), linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +   geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = ybar - 1.96 * se, ymax = ybar + 1.96 * se),                   color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(x = NULL, y = "Control mean", title = "Historical control arms") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12) n_int    <- 70 int_ctrl <- tibble(y = rnorm(n_int, mean = 10, sd = sigma_known))

title: Meta-Analytic-Predictive (MAP) Priors with shrinkr and beastt
text: known SD flat Stage-1 prior, study’s posterior control mean exactly N(ybar, se^2) — reported summaries Stage-1 result, can hand straight shrink(). hierarchical model θ̂g∣θg∼N(θg,seg2),θg∣μ,τ∼N(μ,τ2), \hat\theta_g \mid \theta_g \sim N(\theta_g,\, se_g^2), \qquad \theta_g \mid \mu, \tau \sim N(\mu, \tau^2), vague prior population mean mu weakly-informative half-normal -study SD tau. worth checking tau prior implies differences study means fitting. sample_prior_predictive() draws study effects prior_pairwise_differences() summarizes implied |theta_i - theta_j| (location mu cancels, isolates heterogeneity even though mu vague).  spread looks unreasonable clinical scale, adjust tau prior now. fit, passing study summaries shrink()’s mle / var_matrix interface.
code: hierarchical_priors <- list(   mu  = dist_normal(0, 100),   tau = dist_truncated(dist_normal(0, sigma_known / 4), lower = 0) ) prior_pred <- sample_prior_predictive(hierarchical_priors,                                       n_groups = nrow(hist), n_draws = 2000) plot(prior_pairwise_differences(prior_pred)) fit_map <- shrink(   mle                 = hist$ybar,   var_matrix          = hist$se^2,   hierarchical_priors = hierarchical_priors,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 1000,   seed = 2026, refresh = 0, verbose = FALSE ) summarize_mu_tau(fit_map) #> # A tibble: 3 × 9 #>   parameter      mean     sd      q2.5     q50  q97.5  rhat ess_bulk ess_tail #>   <chr>         <dbl>  <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 mu          10.0    0.152  9.72      10.0    10.3    1.00    5363.    4372. #> 2 tau          0.193  0.151  0.00702    0.163   0.571  1.00    4483.    5013. #> 3 tau_squared  0.0601 0.0939 0.0000493  0.0266  0.326  1.00    4483.    5013.

title: Meta-Analytic-Predictive (MAP) Priors with shrinkr and beastt
text: MAP posterior predictive distribution new study’s control mean. Marginalizing (mu, tau), Normal approximation mean E[mu] variance Var(mu) + E[tau^2] — second term predictive spread heterogeneity, keeps MAP honestly wider simple pooled mean. convenient way read MAP’s strength prior effective sample size: Normal prior mean known SD, sigma^2 / Var(prior) — many control patients prior “worth”.
code: make_map <- function(fit) {   d <- extract_mu_tau(fit)   dist_normal(mean(d$mu), sqrt(var(d$mu) + mean(d$tau_squared))) }  map_prior <- make_map(fit_map) map_prior #> <distribution[1]> #> [1] N(10, 0.083) map_ess <- sigma_known^2 / variance(map_prior) map_ess #> [1] 48.14818

what: Robustify and form the posterior with beastt
title: Meta-Analytic-Predictive (MAP) Priors with shrinkr and beastt
text: robustify_norm() mixes MAP (“informative”) vague component data can overrule prior conflict. Passing MAP’s prior ESS n makes vague component unit-information prior (variance sigma^2); put equal weight two components.  Now combine robust mixture internal control arm. known SD posterior mixture normals, beastt updates mixture weights automatically — -weighting informative component internal data disagree . -borrowing reference simply uses vague component alone.  effective sample size compares posterior variance without borrowing (Pennello & Thompson, 2008): borrowed posterior variance Vb informative n_int * V0 / Vb patients. historical internal data compatible, robust MAP sharpens control posterior lifts effective sample size well 70 internal controls. prior-data conflict informative component lose weight gain shrink toward zero — self-correcting behavior robust mixture provide.
code: rmp         <- robustify_norm(map_prior, n = map_ess, weights = c(0.5, 0.5)) vague_prior <- dist_normal(mix_means(rmp)[["vague"]], mix_sigmas(rmp)[["vague"]])  plot_dist("MAP (informative)" = map_prior,           "Vague component"   = vague_prior,           "Robust mixture"    = rmp) post_borrow <- calc_post_norm(int_ctrl, response = y,                               prior = rmp, internal_sd = sigma_known) post_nobrrw <- calc_post_norm(int_ctrl, response = y,                               prior = vague_prior, internal_sd = sigma_known)  plot_dist("No borrowing"           = post_nobrrw,           "Borrowing (robust MAP)" = post_borrow) ess_post <- n_int * variance(post_nobrrw) / variance(post_borrow)  tibble(   quantity     = c("Posterior mean", "Posterior SD", "Effective sample size"),   no_borrowing = round(c(mean(post_nobrrw), sqrt(variance(post_nobrrw)), n_int), 2),   robust_map   = round(c(mean(post_borrow), sqrt(variance(post_borrow)), ess_post), 2) ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   quantity              no_borrowing robust_map #>   <chr>                        <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Posterior mean                9.66       9.77 #> 2 Posterior SD                  0.24       0.21 #> 3 Effective sample size        70         93.1

code: # Get the hierarchical mean (mu) mu_draws <- draws %>% spread_draws(mu) mu_mean <- mean(mu_draws$mu)  # Combine with Stage 1 samples stage1_draws <- lapply(seq_along(samples_list), function(i) {   data.frame(     region = region_names[i],     .value = samples_list[[i]][,1],     type = "Stage 1"   ) }) %>% bind_rows()  stage2_draws <- theta_tidy %>%   mutate(type = "Stage 2 (Shrunk)")  # Plot side by side bind_rows(stage1_draws, stage2_draws) %>%   ggplot(aes(y = region, x = .value, fill = type)) +   stat_halfeye(alpha = 0.7, position = position_dodge(width = 0.4)) +   geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5, color = "gray50") +   geom_vline(xintercept = mu_mean, linetype = "solid", alpha = 0.8,               color = "darkred", linewidth = 1) +   annotate("text", x = mu_mean, y = 0.5,             label = sprintf("Global mean (μ) = %.2f", mu_mean),            hjust = -0.1, color = "darkred", size = 3.5) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("Stage 1" = "gray70", "Stage 2 (Shrunk)" = "steelblue")) +   labs(     title = "Stage 1 vs Stage 2: Effect of Hierarchical Shrinkage",     subtitle = "Stage 2 estimates are pulled toward the global mean",     x = "Treatment Effect",     y = NULL,     fill = NULL   ) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Working with shrinkr in the Tidy Bayesian Ecosystem
text: ’s typical analysis workflow using tidy principles:
code: # 1. Extract and prepare data analysis_data <- draws %>%   spread_draws(mu, tau, theta[i]) %>%   mutate(region = region_names[i])  # 2. Compute summaries summary_table <- analysis_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   summarise(     mean = mean(theta),     median = median(theta),     sd = sd(theta),     q025 = quantile(theta, 0.025),     q975 = quantile(theta, 0.975),     prob_positive = mean(theta > 0),     prob_clinically_important = mean(theta > 0.3),     .groups = "drop"   ) %>%   arrange(desc(median))  print(summary_table) #> # A tibble: 5 × 8 #>   region   mean median    sd    q025  q975 prob_positive prob_clinically_impor…¹ #>   <chr>   <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl>         <dbl>                   <dbl> #> 1 Central 0.543  0.530 0.175  0.227  0.911         1.000                   0.927 #> 2 South   0.512  0.501 0.169  0.203  0.870         0.999                   0.908 #> 3 North   0.428  0.428 0.166  0.106  0.756         0.993                   0.794 #> 4 East    0.377  0.380 0.168  0.0203 0.709         0.983                   0.690 #> 5 West    0.110  0.117 0.212 -0.318  0.481         0.695                   0.207 #> # ℹ abbreviated name: ¹​prob_clinically_important  # 3. Create advanced figure library(patchwork)  p1 <- analysis_data %>%   ggplot(aes(y = reorder(region, theta), x = theta)) +   stat_halfeye(.width = c(0.66, 0.95), fill = "steelblue") +   geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +   labs(     title = "A. Regional Treatment Effects",     x = "Effect Size",     y = NULL   )  p2 <- analysis_data %>%   dplyr::ungroup() %>%   dplyr::select(mu, tau, .draw) %>%   dplyr::distinct() %>%   tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = c(mu, tau), names_to = "name", values_to = "value") %>%   ggplot(aes(x = value, fill = name)) +   stat_halfeye(alpha = 0.7) +   facet_wrap(~name, scales = "free", labeller = label_both) +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +   labs(     title = "B. Hyperparameters",     x = "Value",     y = "Density"   ) +   theme(legend.position = "none")  p3 <- analysis_data %>%   dplyr::ungroup() %>%   dplyr::select(.draw, region, theta) %>%   compare_levels(theta, by = region) %>%   ggplot(aes(y = region, x = theta)) +   stat_halfeye(fill = "coral", alpha = 0.7) +   geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", alpha = 0.5) +   labs(     title = "C. Pairwise Regional Comparisons",     x = "Difference in Effect Size",     y = NULL   )  p4 <- analysis_data %>%   dplyr::ungroup() %>%   dplyr::select(.draw, mu, tau) %>%   dplyr::distinct() %>%   ggplot(aes(x = mu, y = tau)) +   geom_hex(bins = 30) +   stat_ellipse(level = 0.95, color = "red", linewidth = 1) +   scale_fill_viridis_c() +   labs(     title = "D. Hyperparameter Correlation",     x = expression(mu~"(global mean)"),     y = expression(tau~"(heterogeneity)")   )  (p1 + p2) / (p3 + p4) +   plot_annotation(     title = "Complete Bayesian Shrinkage Analysis",     subtitle = sprintf(       "Global effect: %.2f [%.2f, %.2f] | Heterogeneity (tau): %.2f",       median(analysis_data$mu),       quantile(analysis_data$mu, 0.025),       quantile(analysis_data$mu, 0.975),       median(analysis_data$tau)     )   )

code: # Classify effects into categories theta_tidy %>%   group_by(region) %>%   summarise(     prob_harm = mean(.value < -0.1),     prob_null = mean(abs(.value) < 0.1),     prob_small_benefit = mean(.value > 0.1 & .value < 0.3),     prob_large_benefit = mean(.value > 0.3),     .groups = "drop"   ) %>%   arrange(desc(prob_large_benefit)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   region  prob_harm prob_null prob_small_benefit prob_large_benefit #>   <chr>       <dbl>     <dbl>              <dbl>              <dbl> #> 1 Central   0          0.004              0.069               0.927 #> 2 South     0          0.0045             0.0872              0.908 #> 3 North     0.00175    0.0217             0.183               0.794 #> 4 East      0.00325    0.0488             0.258               0.690 #> 5 West      0.167      0.309              0.317               0.207  # Visualize classification theta_tidy %>%   mutate(     category = case_when(       .value < -0.1 ~ "Harm",       abs(.value) < 0.1 ~ "Null",       .value > 0.1 & .value < 0.3 ~ "Small Benefit",       .value > 0.3 ~ "Large Benefit"     )   ) %>%   count(region, category) %>%   group_by(region) %>%   mutate(probability = n / sum(n)) %>%   ggplot(aes(x = probability, y = region, fill = category)) +   geom_col(position = "stack") +   scale_fill_manual(     values = c(       "Harm" = "red",       "Null" = "gray",       "Small Benefit" = "lightblue",       "Large Benefit" = "darkblue"     )   ) +   labs(     title = "Classification of Treatment Effects",     x = "Probability",     y = NULL,     fill = "Effect Category"   ) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Modular Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Models
text: Stage 1: Fit Bayesian model (e.g., Stan, NIMBLE, JAGS) without shrinkage using flat/uninformative priors parameters interest. Extract posterior draws subgroup-specific parameters (θg\theta_g, treatment effect estimand interest subgroup g=1,…Gg = 1, \ldots G). Stage 2: Approximate subgroup posteriors mixture multivariate normals, fit hierarchical model across groups borrow strength (“shrink”). modular approach lets separate shrinkage first model—ideal simulation pipelines, sensitivity checks, Bayesian data borrowing. especially useful? mixture approximation makes shrinkr particularly valuable situations typical two-stage approach assuming normal likelihood Stage 1 posteriors unlikely hold—example, small subgroups, rare events, skewed multimodal posteriors, complex survival models. capturing non-normality Gaussian mixture, Stage 2 shrinkage better reflects actual uncertainty Stage 1. approach mathematically justified marginalization—see Mathematical Foundation full derivation.

code: # On CRAN install.packages("shrinkr") # installs current release   # Install from GSK-Biostatistics repository # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("GSK-Biostatistics/shrinkr") # installs developmental version  # Install from internal repository or local source # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_local("path/to/shrinkr")  # Or install dependencies first install.packages(c("rstan", "distributional", "mclust", "posterior"))

title: Modular Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Models
code: library(shrinkr) library(distributional)  # Step 1: Prepare posterior samples from your Stage 1 model # Here we simulate posterior draws for 4 subgroups as an example. # theta_g represents the subgroup-specific treatment effect for group g. # In practice, these come from Stan, NIMBLE, JAGS, brms, etc. # IMPORTANT: Use flat/uninformative priors on theta in Stage 1 set.seed(1104) samples <- list(   group1 = matrix(rnorm(2000, mean = 0.0, sd = 0.5), ncol = 1),   group2 = matrix(rnorm(2000, mean = 0.5, sd = 0.5), ncol = 1),   group3 = matrix(rnorm(2000, mean = 1.0, sd = 0.5), ncol = 1),   group4 = matrix(rnorm(2000, mean = 0.3, sd = 0.6), ncol = 1) )  # Step 2: Fit mixture approximation (up to K_max = 3 Gaussian components) # K_max controls the maximum number of mixture components. mclust selects # the best K (<= K_max) via BIC. Default is 5; 3 is often sufficient. mix <- fit_mixture(samples, K_max = 3, verbose = TRUE)  # Step 3: Apply hierarchical shrinkage with custom priors priors <- list(   mu = dist_normal(0, 5),   tau = dist_truncated(dist_student_t(3, 0, 1), lower = 0) )  # Check what the priors imply about pairwise differences prior_pred <- sample_prior_predictive(priors, n_groups = 4, n_draws = 2000) pw <- prior_pairwise_differences(prior_pred) plot(pw)  # density of |theta_i - theta_j|  fit <- shrink(   mixture = mix,   hierarchical_priors = priors,   chains = 4,   iter = 2000,   warmup = 1000 )  # Step 4: Examine results summary(fit) plot(fit)

title: Modular Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Models
text: shrinkr supports wide range priors distributional package: Standard families: Normal, Student-t, Cauchy, Lognormal Heavy-tailed: Inverse-Gamma, Half-Cauchy, Half-t Uniform priors bounded heterogeneity Truncated distributions constraining parameter ranges mu tau
code: # Examples of different tau priors priors <- list(   mu = dist_normal(0, 5),   tau = dist_truncated(dist_normal(0, 2.5), lower = 0)  # Half-normal )  # Or with half-t for heavier tails priors <- list(   mu = dist_normal(0, 5),   tau = dist_truncated(dist_student_t(3, 0, 1), lower = 0)  # Half-t )  # Truncated mu (e.g., constrain global mean to be positive) priors <- list(   mu = dist_truncated(dist_normal(0, 5), lower = 0),   tau = dist_truncated(dist_normal(0, 2.5), lower = 0) )  # Spike-and-slab prior on tau for testing homogeneity priors <- list(   mu = dist_normal(0, 5),   tau = dist_truncated(prior_spike_slab(spike_prob = 0.5, spike_scale = 0.01, slab_scale = 1), lower = 0) )

title: Modular Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Models
text: Full posterior samples (recommended): Provides mixture approximation captures posterior uncertainty Point estimates + variance: full posteriors unavailable
code: fit <- shrink(mixture = mix, hierarchical_priors = priors) fit <- shrink(   mle = c(0.5, 1.2, -0.3, 0.8),   var_matrix = c(0.1, 0.15, 0.12, 0.09),  # Vector assumes independent components   #can also be variance-covariance matrix   hierarchical_priors = priors )

title: Modular Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Models
text: fitting, check implications prior choices:
code: # Check prior implications prior_pred <- sample_prior_predictive(   hierarchical_priors = priors,   n_groups = 4,   n_draws = 1000 )  # Visualize hyperparameter and theta distributions plot(prior_pred)  # Check implied spread summary(prior_pred)  # implied_range: for each draw, this is max(theta) - min(theta) across groups. # It tells you how spread out the subgroup effects are under your prior. median(prior_pred$implied_range)  # Typical range across groups quantile(prior_pred$implied_range, c(0.025, 0.975))  # 95% CI  # Prior predictive pairwise differences: |theta_i - theta_j| # This is recommended for calibrating priors — see whether your prior # implies plausible subgroup differences on the clinical scale. pw <- prior_pairwise_differences(prior_pred) print(pw) plot(pw)                      # Pooled density plot(pw, by_pair = TRUE)      # Violin per pair

title: Modular Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Models
text: two-stage approach mathematically justified marginalization. key insight Stage 1 prior θ must uninformative (flat/improper) equivalence hold.

title: Modular Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Models
text: standard hierarchical model, joint posterior : π(θ,μ,τ,ψ|D)∝L(θ,ψ|D)⋅π(θ|μ,τ)⋅π(μ)⋅π(τ)⋅π(ψ)\pi(\theta, \mu, \tau, \psi | D) \propto L(\theta, \psi | D) \cdot \pi(\theta | \mu, \tau) \cdot \pi(\mu) \cdot \pi(\tau) \cdot \pi(\psi) : - θ\theta = subgroup-specific parameters interest - μ,τ\mu, \tau = hierarchical mean variance parameters - ψ\psi = nuisance parameters likelihood - DD = observed data Marginalizing ψ\psi: π(θ,μ,τ|D)∝[∫L(θ,ψ|D)π(ψ)dψ]⋅π(θ|μ,τ)⋅π(μ)⋅π(τ)\pi(\theta, \mu, \tau | D) \propto \left[\int L(\theta, \psi | D) \pi(\psi) d\psi \right] \cdot \pi(\theta | \mu, \tau) \cdot \pi(\mu) \cdot \pi(\tau)

title: Modular Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Models
text: Stage 1 uses flat/uninformative prior θ: π1(θ|D)∝∫L(θ,ψ|D)π(ψ)dψ\pi_1(\theta | D) \propto \int L(\theta, \psi | D) \pi(\psi) d\psi posterior π(θ)∝1\pi(\theta) \propto 1 (flat prior). Notice matches bracketed term . Stage 2 treats Stage 1 posterior “data”: π(θ,μ,τ|D)∝π1(θ|D)⋅π(θ|μ,τ)⋅π(μ)⋅π(τ)\pi(\theta, \mu, \tau | D) \propto \pi_1(\theta | D) \cdot \pi(\theta | \mu, \tau) \cdot \pi(\mu) \cdot \pi(\tau)

title: Modular Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Models
text: equivalence holds : Stage 1 prior flat: π(θ)∝1\pi(\theta) \propto 1, doesn’t appear Stage 1 posterior shrinkage comes Stage 2: hierarchical prior π(θ|μ,τ)\pi(\theta | \mu, \tau) applied Stage 2 “double priors”: don’t π1(θ)\pi_1(\theta) π(θ|μ,τ)\pi(\theta | \mu, \tau) competing

title: Modular Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Models
text: Always use flat/weakly informative priors Stage 1: Apply hierarchical structure Stage 2: shrinkr package implements approximating π(θ|D)\pi(\theta | D) mixture normals Stage 2, enabling efficient exploration different hierarchical priors without refitting Stage 1 model.
code: # Stan: Don't specify a prior (defaults to improper uniform) parameters {   vector[n_groups] theta;  // No prior = flat prior }  # JAGS: Use very wide prior theta[g] ~ dnorm(0, 1e-6)  # Precision = 1e-6 means very wide  # NIMBLE: Similar to JAGS theta[g] ~ dnorm(0, sd = 1000) priors <- list(   mu = dist_normal(0, 5),      # This is the ONLY prior on location   tau = dist_truncated(dist_student_t(3, 0, 1), lower = 0) )  fit <- shrink(mixture = mix, hierarchical_priors = priors)

title: Modular Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Models
text: Stage 1 posterior π1(θ|D)\pi_1(\theta | D) can arbitrarily complex — skewed, multimodal, heavy-tailed — depending model data. need tractable density Stan can evaluate efficiently log-likelihood. mixture multivariate normals natural choice : Universal approximation: continuous density can approximated arbitrary precision finite mixture normals (given enough components). classical result density estimation. Closed-form log-density: component contributes multi_normal_cholesky_lpdf term Stan evaluates analytically — numerical integration needed. Automatic model selection: mclust selects number components KK covariance structure via BIC, user need specify manually. K_max argument sets upper bound (default 5); practice, 2-5 components suffice posterior shapes. Captures non-normality: Unlike point-estimate-plus-variance approach (implicitly assumes normal posterior), mixture path faithfully represents skewness, heavy tails, multimodality Stage 1. assess sensitivity K_max? general, BIC-selected KK stable KmaxK_{\text{max}} large enough. can check comparing fit_mixture(..., K_max = 3) vs K_max = 5 vs K_max = 8 inspecting QQ plots. selected KK QQ diagnostics unchanged, result robust. function fit_mixture() returns selected KK BIC comparison.

code: set.seed(1) draws <- data.frame(   mu = rnorm(20, 0.2, 0.05),   tau = abs(rnorm(20, 0.3, 0.03)),   `theta[1]` = rnorm(20, 0.0, 0.1),   `theta[2]` = rnorm(20, 0.3, 0.1),   `theta[3]` = rnorm(20, 0.5, 0.1),   check.names = FALSE ) draws$tau_squared <- draws$tau^2 fit <- list(   fit = posterior::as_draws_df(draws),   data = list(     G = 3, K = 1, centered = FALSE,     vars = c("group1", "group2", "group3"),     quantiles = data.frame(       q2.5 = c(-0.20, 0.10, 0.30),       q50 = c(0.00, 0.30, 0.50),       q97.5 = c(0.20, 0.50, 0.70)     )   ),   summary = posterior::summarise_draws(     posterior::as_draws_df(draws),     "mean", "sd",     ~posterior::quantile2(., probs = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))   ),   diagnostics = list(n_divergent = 0, max_treedepth = 0, n_leapfrog = 0) ) class(fit) <- "shrinkr_fit" all_draws <- posterior::as_draws_df(fit) posterior::variables(all_draws) #> [1] "mu"          "tau"         "theta[1]"    "theta[2]"    "theta[3]"    #> [6] "tau_squared" theta_draws <- posterior::as_draws_df(fit, variables = c("theta[1]", "theta[2]"))

what: Fit Gaussian mixture models to posterior samples — fit_mixture
title: Fit Gaussian mixture models to posterior samples — fit_mixture
text: Fits multivariate Gaussian mixture model (GMM) jointly across supplied variables using mclust. function intended approximating posterior draws Bayesian models produces tidy component table suitable visualization shrinkage modeling.

title: Fit Gaussian mixture models to posterior samples — fit_mixture
code: fit_mixture(samples, K_max = 5L, verbose = FALSE, model_names = NULL, ...)

title: Fit Gaussian mixture models to posterior samples — fit_mixture
text: samples Posterior samples one following formats: Data frame matrix (recommended): column represents one group/variable shrink, rows posterior draws. Column names used variable labels. Example: output posterior::as_draws_df() matrix columns = groups. Named list vectors: list element contains posterior samples one group (numeric vector). Names used variable labels. vectors must length. Named list matrices: list element matrix posterior samples one group. univariate parameters, single-column matrices (n × 1). matrices must number rows (draws). least two groups/variables required hierarchical shrinkage. Non-numeric columns data frames automatically dropped. Rows missing values removed fitting. K_max Integer \((\ge 1)\). Maximum number mixture components consider model selection BIC. Internally capped n - 1 stability. verbose Logical. TRUE, progress diagnostic messages printed. model_names Optional character vector mclust covariance model codes consider (e.g., "EII", "VVV", etc.). NULL (default), models appropriate data dimension considered mclust. convenience wrapper modelNames mclust::Mclust(). ... Additional arguments forwarded mclust::Mclust(), prior, initialization, control = mclust::emControl(), warn, verbose. Use fine-tune EM control, priors, initialization.

title: Fit Gaussian mixture models to posterior samples — fit_mixture
text: list class "shrinkr_mixture" containing: components — data frame columns: group, component, variable, weight, mean, sd (marginal standard deviations). K — number mixture components selected. vars — character vector variable names. weights — vector component weights (mixing proportions). covs — list component covariance matrices (p × p ). model_name — selected mclust covariance structure (e.g., "VVV"). bic — Bayesian Information Criterion fitted model. n_samples — number samples used fitting. n_vars — number variables. mclust_fit — complete mclust model object (advanced use). diagnostics — list sample size details, removed rows, quality warnings.

title: Fit Gaussian mixture models to posterior samples — fit_mixture
text: Requires least two variables; shrinkage across single variable meaningful. Rows missing values removed fitting. Component weights normalized sum one. sd column reports marginal standard deviations (square roots diagonal entries) component covariance matrix.

title: Fit Gaussian mixture models to posterior samples — fit_mixture
text: mclust parameterizes component covariances via eigen-decomposition offers set model families controlling volume (V), shape (S), orientation (O). Common codes include (non-exhaustive): Spherical "EII": equal volume, spherical "VII": variable volume, spherical Diagonal "EEI": equal volume & shape (axis-aligned) "VEI": variable volume, equal shape "EVI": equal volume, variable shape "VVI": variable volume & shape Ellipsoidal (full covariance) "EEE": equal volume, shape, orientation "EEV": equal volume & shape, variable orientation "VEV": variable volume, equal shape, variable orientation "VVV": variable volume, shape, orientation (flexible) model_names = NULL (default), mclust::Mclust() selects among models appropriate data dimension via BIC. practice, lets data decide parsimonious structures (e.g., "EII", "VVI") fully flexible ones (e.g., "VVV"). can restrict expand search space supplying model_names.

title: Fit Gaussian mixture models to posterior samples — fit_mixture
code: set.seed(1) samples <- list(   group1 = matrix(rnorm(100, 0.0, 0.5), ncol = 1),   group2 = matrix(rnorm(100, 0.5, 0.5), ncol = 1),   group3 = matrix(rnorm(100, 1.0, 0.5), ncol = 1) ) mix <- fit_mixture(samples, K_max = 2, verbose = FALSE) summary(mix) #> == Mixture Model Summary ============================ #>  #> Model: XII  #> Components: 1  #> Log-likelihood: -205.559  #> BIC: -429.539  #> Parameters: 4  #>  #> Component weights: #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>   component weight #>       <int>  <dbl> #> 1         1      1 #>  #> Variable-wise summaries (weighted across components): #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   variable weighted_mean weighted_sd range_mean range_sd #>   <chr>            <dbl>       <dbl>      <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 group1          0.0544       0.480          0        0 #> 2 group2          0.481        0.480          0        0 #> 3 group3          1.01         0.480          0        0 #>  #> -----------------------------------------------------

Default FALSE. applies type = "shrinkage". show_intervals Logical; show credible intervals pre-shrunk post-shrunk estimates? Default 3. arrow_alpha Numeric; transparency arrows (0-1). Default 0.6. applies show_arrows = TRUE. dodge_width Numeric; horizontal spacing pre-shrunk post-shrunk estimates side--side display. title: Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Model — shrink
text: mixture shrinkr_mixture object fit_mixture(). Contains Gaussian mixture approximation Stage 1 posteriors. Either mixture mle var_matrix must provided. mle Numeric vector group point estimates. Used mixture NULL. var_matrix Numeric vector variances (length G) covariance matrix (G × G). Required mle provided. hierarchical_priors Named list mu tau priors distributional objects. Defaults weakly informative priors: mu: Global mean, dist_normal(0, 5) tau: -group SD, dist_truncated(dist_normal(0, 2.5), lower = 0) Supported distributions mu: Normal, Student-t, mixture priors, truncated versions (e.g., dist_truncated(dist_normal(0, 5), lower = 0)). Supported distributions tau: Normal (truncated), Student-t (truncated), Cauchy (truncated), Lognormal, Gamma, Inverse-Gamma, Exponential, Uniform, mixture priors (including spike--slab via prior_spike_slab()). centered Logical; use centered (TRUE) non-centered (FALSE, default) parameterization. Non-centered efficient heterogeneity small. verbose Logical; print progress messages (default TRUE). ... Additional arguments passed rstan::sampling(): chains: Number chains (default 4) iter: Iterations per chain (default 2000) warmup: Warmup iterations (default iter/2) cores: Cores parallel sampling seed: Random seed control: List sampler controls (e.g., list(adapt_delta = 0.95))

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Model — shrink
text: Hierarchical model (Stage 2): $$\theta_g \mid \mu, \tau \sim \text{Normal}(\mu, \tau^2), \quad g = 1, \ldots, G$$ $$\mu \sim \pi(\mu)$$ $$\tau \sim \pi(\tau)$$ Stage 1 likelihood (Gaussian mixture approximation): $$\theta_g \mid D_g \sim q_g(\theta_g) \approx \sum_{k=1}^K w_k \, \text{MVN}(\mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$ Full posterior: $$\pi(\theta, \mu, \tau \mid D) \propto   \left[\prod_{g=1}^G q_g(\theta_g)\right]   \left[\prod_{g=1}^G \text{Normal}(\theta_g \mid \mu, \tau^2)\right]   \pi(\mu) \pi(\tau)$$ \(q_g(\theta_g)\) approximates Stage 1 posterior group \(g\).

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Model — shrink
text: Fixed: Hierarchical distribution: \(\theta_g \mid \mu, \tau \sim \text{Normal}(\mu, \tau^2)\) Flexible: Hyperpriors \(\pi(\mu)\) \(\pi(\tau)\): Normal, Student-t, Cauchy, Lognormal, Gamma, Inverse-Gamma, Exponential, Uniform, mixtures (including spike--slab), truncated versions Stage 1 posteriors: Can non-Normal (handled mixture approximation)

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Model — shrink
text: Stage 1 must use flat/uninformative priors \(\theta_g\) Ensures two-stage = one-stage hierarchical model Stan: specify prior (defaults flat) JAGS/NIMBLE: Use wide priors Verify mixture quality: plot(mixture, draws = samples) Check density overlays QQ plots Poor approximation → biased shrinkage Check prior implications: sample_prior_predictive(hierarchical_priors) Understand priors imply fitting Avoid prior-data conflicts Minimum 2 groups required heterogeneity estimation

title: Bayesian Hierarchical Shrinkage Model — shrink
text: Half-Normal: dist_truncated(dist_normal(0, s), lower = 0) - Weakly informative Half-t: dist_truncated(dist_student_t(df, 0, s), lower = 0) - Heavier tails Half-Cauchy: dist_truncated(dist_cauchy(0, s), lower = 0) - diffuse Uniform: dist_uniform(0, U) - Bounded heterogeneity Inverse-Gamma: dist_inverse_gamma(, b) - Traditional choice See vignette("getting_started") complete workflow, vignette("brms_integration") real examples, package README mathematical justification.

title: Linear combinations of theta — theta_contrasts
text: fit shrinkr_fit object shrink(). contrast_matrix numeric matrix L ncol(L) = G (number groups) nrow(L) = M (number contrasts). row defines one linear combination: $$contrast_i = L_{i1}\theta_1 + L_{i2}\theta_2 + \ldots + L_{iG}\theta_G$$ labels Optional character vector length M name contrasts. NULL, uses "contrast1", "contrast2", etc.

code: set.seed(1) draws <- data.frame(   mu = rnorm(20, 0.2, 0.05),   tau = abs(rnorm(20, 0.3, 0.03)),   `theta[1]` = rnorm(20, 0.0, 0.1),   `theta[2]` = rnorm(20, 0.3, 0.1),   `theta[3]` = rnorm(20, 0.5, 0.1),   check.names = FALSE ) draws$tau_squared <- draws$tau^2 fit <- list(   fit = posterior::as_draws_df(draws),   data = list(     G = 3, K = 1, centered = FALSE,     vars = c("group1", "group2", "group3"),     quantiles = data.frame(       q2.5 = c(-0.20, 0.10, 0.30),       q50 = c(0.00, 0.30, 0.50),       q97.5 = c(0.20, 0.50, 0.70)     )   ),   summary = posterior::summarise_draws(     posterior::as_draws_df(draws),     "mean", "sd",     ~posterior::quantile2(., probs = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))   ),   diagnostics = list(n_divergent = 0, max_treedepth = 0, n_leapfrog = 0) ) class(fit) <- "shrinkr_fit" L <- matrix(c(-1, 1, 0), nrow = 1) contrast <- theta_contrasts(fit, L, labels = "group2_vs_group1") posterior::summarise_draws(contrast) #> # A tibble: 1 × 10 #>   variable         mean median    sd    mad     q5   q95  rhat ess_bulk ess_tail #>   <chr>           <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 group2_vs_grou… 0.296  0.304 0.118 0.0756 0.0805 0.436  1.06     17.1     20.4

Reworked documentation examples lightweight examples executable self-contained. Replaced unnecessary \dontrun{} blocks executable examples \donttest{} appropriate. title: shrinkr 0.4.4
text: New vignette: vignette("map_prior_with_beastt"). Demonstrates build robust meta-analytic-predictive (MAP) prior pairing shrinkr beastt package. shrinkr runs hierarchical meta-analysis across historical control arms returns MAP distributional object, beastt robustifies (robustify_norm()) combines internal control arm (calc_post_norm()) form control posterior report effective sample size. worked example uses continuous outcome known SD, prior calibrated sample_prior_predictive() / prior_pairwise_differences().

title: shrinkr 0.4.3
text: Fixed inverse-gamma prior translation R/utils.R. branch handling dist_inverse_gamma priors tau reading params$shape params$scale, distributional::parameters() returns fields s r. check !.null(params$shape) && !.null(params$scale) always FALSE, branch silently failed downstream code relied format-string regex fallback rescue parsing. End--end posteriors correct (fallback happened parse format() output identically explicit branch produced), code path fragile. branch now reads params$s / params$r parameters(tau_raw) (unwrapped distribution, possibly-truncated wrapper) dispatches correctly without needing fallback. Fixed uniform prior translation R/utils.R. dist_uniform branch reading tau_raw$min tau_raw$max, distributional stores bounds l u. pattern IG bug: branch silently failed, format-string fallback rescued . branch now reads tau_raw$l / tau_raw$u dispatches correctly.

title: shrinkr 0.4.3
text: Removed unused stan_code() generic methods R/prior_system.R. generic 9 distributional-family methods vestiges earlier, abandoned design Stan code assembled runtime prior strings. shrinkr’s actual workflow handles prior dispatch internally inside precompiled Stan model (inst/stan/stage2_shrinkage.stan) via integer prior codes parameter arrays. dead code path never reached runtime; removing drops ~120 lines source, 9 S3method entries NAMESPACE, 10 .Rd files man/. prior_mixture() prior_spike_slab() remain unchanged. Removed format-string regex fallback R/utils.R. Roughly 85 lines across mu tau translation paths used format(prior) string parsing last-resort dispatch explicit class-based branches missed. inverse-gamma fix , every supported prior now dispatches via class-based checks, making fallback redundant. Removing leaves cleaner, transparent failure mode: unsupported prior raises clear error rather silently passing regex parser.

title: shrinkr 0.4.1
text: Fixed Stan model sampling speed regression: Restored std_normal() prior inactive parameterization vector (z centered, theta_c non-centered). 0.4.0, inactive vector sampling statement, giving implicit improper uniform prior. caused Stan’s NUTS sampler waste effort exploring unbounded space, significantly slowing sampling. fix constrains inactive vector without affecting model.

title: shrinkr 0.4.0
text: Removed parse_priors(): legacy formula-based prior interface removed. Use distributional objects (e.g., dist_normal(), dist_student_t()) directly shrink() via hierarchical_priors argument. Removed shrinkage_factor Stan generated quantities. quantity (tau^2 / (tau^2 + 1)) misleading assumed standardized Stage 1 variance. Use tau_squared directly heterogeneity assessment. Removed group column fit_mixture() output. column always 1L served purpose. variable column identifies subgroups. prior_mixture() prior_spike_slab() now return native distributional objects via distributional::dist_mixture(). replaces previous custom dist_mixture class. distributional operations (sampling, density, format, quantiles) work automatically. Code accessed $components $weights fields directly need updating. prior_spike_slab() must truncated tau: Since tau scale parameter, spike--slab priors must wrapped dist_truncated(..., lower = 0). sample_prior_predictive() now errors (rather warns) tau draws negative. Example: tau = dist_truncated(prior_spike_slab(), lower = 0).

title: shrinkr 0.4.0
text: Mixture prior support now works end--end: prior_mixture() prior_spike_slab() now correctly flow Stan mu tau priors. Previously, exported wired .coerce_priors_to_stan() pipeline. Truncated mu priors: mu now supports truncation via dist_truncated(dist_normal(0, 5), lower = 0). enables constraining global mean scientifically meaningful range. prior_pairwise_differences(): New function compute prior-implied distribution |theta_i - theta_j| prior predictive samples. Includes print() plot() S3 methods. pooled view uses skyblue density (matching hyperparameter panel) -pair view uses lightcoral violins (matching theta panel). Useful calibrating priors following recommendation inspect pairwise difference distributions fitting. Re-exported as_draws_df() posterior package users can call as_draws_df(fit) without explicitly loading posterior.

title: shrinkr 0.4.0
text: Safe Cholesky factorization: .as_chol_factor() now uses jitter--retry strategy (1e-10 1e-4) Matrix::nearPD() fallback instead passing non-lower-triangular eigen decomposition Stan. old fallback silently produce invalid cholesky_factor_cov input. Stan model longer exposes unused parameters: inactive parameterization (z centered, theta_c non-centered) constrained std_normal() prior doesn’t appear meaningful parameter output. Fixed vignette references: Standardized underscore naming (getting_started, brms_integration, tidy_bayesian_workflow, federated_learning) throughout R docs, man pages, vignettes, README. Removed references nonexistent vignettes (mathematical-foundation, two_stage_demo, tidybayes-integration). Mixture component extraction handles truncated normals: internal .extract_normal_params() helper correctly unwraps dist_truncated(dist_normal()) extract underlying mean sd Stan mixture priors. needed dist_truncated(prior_spike_slab(), lower = 0) work.
